era_context: The Second Crusade was launched in 1147, representing a major mobilization of European military and ecclesiastical resources. The Zengid dynasty under Nur ad-Din was consolidating power in Syria following the death of his father Imad ad-Din Zengi in 1146. In Europe, the High Middle Ages were characterized by feudal fragmentation, monastic reform, and the rise of scholasticism. The Jin Dynasty controlled northern China; the Southern Song Dynasty ruled the south. The Islamic world was experiencing intellectual and cultural flourishing. Most of the world outside Europe, the Islamic Mediterranean, and East Asia remains poorly documented in surviving written sources.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and North America (no European documentation; local records sparse)
Eastern Europe and the Steppe (sparse written sources)

events.title: Launch of the Second Crusade
events.description: The Second Crusade was formally launched in 1147 by Pope Eugenius III, with Bernard of Clairvaux as its principal preacher. The campaign was called in response to the fall of the County of Edessa to Zengid forces in 1144. Major European powers participated, including the Kingdom of France under Louis VII and the Holy Roman Empire under Conrad III. The Crusade also involved local Crusader states in the Levant (Kingdom of Jerusalem, County of Tripoli, Principality of Antioch). The campaign represented one of the largest mobilizations of European military resources in the 12th century. Unlike the First Crusade, which had been a largely uncoordinated movement of various groups, the Second Crusade was organized by the papacy and involved the participation of crowned monarchs.
key_figures: Conrad III of the Holy Roman Empire (military leader)
Eleanor of Aquitaine (participant, wife of Louis VII)

sources.name: Odo of Deuil, De Profectione Ludovici VII in Orientem
William of Tyre, Historia Belli Sacri
Bernard of Clairvaux, Letters and sermons

events.certainty_note: The launch of the Second Crusade in 1147 is extensively documented in both Western and Islamic sources. The date and basic facts are well-established.
cross_references: 1144-001 (Fall of Edessa, catalyst for the Crusade)

events.title: Nur ad-Din Consolidates Power Following His Father's Death
events.description: In 1147, Nur ad-Din Zengi (r. 1146–1174) was consolidating his power following the death of his father Imad ad-Din Zengi in 1146. Nur ad-Din faced challenges from rival factions within the Zengid state and from external enemies, including the Crusaders and other Muslim powers. By 1147, he had secured his position in Aleppo and was beginning to extend his influence toward Damascus and other parts of Syria. His consolidation was accompanied by administrative reforms, the promotion of Islamic orthodoxy, and military reorganization. Nur ad-Din's rise marked the beginning of a new phase in Islamic history, characterized by the unification of the Levant under a single strong ruler.
events.certainty_note: Nur ad-Din's succession and consolidation of power in 1147 is well-documented in Islamic chronicles. The process was gradual, spanning several years from 1146 onward.

events.title: Almohad Expansion in the Iberian Peninsula and North Africa
events.description: In 1147, the Almohad dynasty, a puritanical Islamic reform movement originating in North Africa, continued its expansion into the Iberian Peninsula and the Maghreb. The Almohads had begun their conquest of Al-Andalus in 1147, gradually replacing the fragmented Taifa kingdoms with a more unified Islamic state. This expansion was part of a broader Almohad movement to reform Islamic society and resist Christian expansion in Iberia. The Almohad conquest of Al-Andalus would continue over the next several decades, culminating in the consolidation of Almohad rule by the late 12th century. The Almohad movement represented a significant shift in Islamic civilization, emphasizing religious orthodoxy and military organization.

sources.name: Al-Marrākushī, Mu'jib fi talkhīs akhbār al-Maghrib (The Expeditious in Summarizing the History of the Maghreb)

events.certainty_note: The Almohad expansion in 1147 is documented in Islamic chronicles. The date marks the beginning of the Almohad conquest of Al-Andalus, which continued over several decades.
cross_references: 1212-001 (Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa, Almohad defeat)

disconfirming_evidence: The Second Crusade is often portrayed as a response to the fall of Edessa in 1144, but modern scholarship suggests that the Crusade was also motivated by broader ecclesiastical and political interests in Europe. Pope Eugenius III and Bernard of Clairvaux had their own agendas, which included the promotion of papal authority and the reform of the Church. The Crusade was not simply a military response to Islamic expansion but a complex political and religious movement. Additionally, the characterization of the Almohad expansion as a unified 'conquest' oversimplifies the complex political dynamics of Al-Andalus and the Maghreb, where local rulers, Christian kingdoms, and Islamic factions all played significant roles.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1147 is dominated by Crusade-centric Western sources, particularly Odo of Deuil's contemporary account and Bernard of Clairvaux's letters and sermons. These sources reflect the perspective of the Western Church and the French monarchy. Islamic sources (Ibn al-Athir, Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani) provide alternative perspectives but were written decades after the events. The experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-combatants are largely absent. The Almohad expansion is documented in Islamic chronicles but is often overshadowed by the Crusade narrative in Western historiography. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and most of Asia are absent from surviving written records available to modern historians.

graph_edges.note: The launch of the Second Crusade in 1147 led to the siege of Damascus in 1148.
Nur ad-Din's consolidation in 1147 continued into 1148, establishing the Zengid state as the dominant Islamic power in the Levant.
The Second Crusade's launch and Nur ad-Din's consolidation of power were simultaneous processes shaping the Levantine balance of power.
The Almohad expansion in 1147 eventually led to the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212, where the Almohads were defeated by Christian kingdoms.
